content.problemStatement:
An array AAA is called weird if it can be sorted in non-decreasing order by applying the given operation any number of times:


Select any index iii (1‚Č§i‚Č§‚ą£A‚ą£)(1 \le i \le |A|)(1‚Č§i‚Č§‚ą£A‚ą£) and set Ai:=‚ąíAiA_i := -A_iAi‚Äč:=‚ąíAi‚Äč.
For example: A=[2,1,3]A = [2, 1, 3]A=[2,1,3] is weird since after applying the operation at i=1i = 1i=1, AAA becomes [‚ąí2,1,3][-2, 1, 3][‚ąí2,1,3] which is sorted.

JJ has a permutation PPP of length NNN. He wants to find the number of subarrays of PPP which are weird. Can you help him?


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT ‚ÄĒ the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN ‚ÄĒ the size of the permutation PPP.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,‚Ä¶,PNP_1, P_2, \dots, P_NP1‚Äč,P2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,PN‚Äč denoting the permutation PPP.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of weird subarrays of PPP.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2‚čÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚čÖ105.
